critic_raw:
The ensemble combines XGBoost on structured features with logistic regression on TF-IDF clinical note features, fused via a fixed equal-weight average. Equal-weight averaging is not a principled ensemble strategy — it assumes the two component models have identical predictive scale and error distributions on the target variable, which is empirically unlikely when one model uses dense structured features and the other uses high-dimensional sparse text features. Without learned ensemble weights or stacking, the ensemble cannot adapt to cases where one modality is more informative, and the fixed combination may underperform a properly weighted or stacked ensemble while also underperforming the stronger individual component.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on structured data only, which is not a comparable baseline to the full ensemble. The ensemble has two informational advantages over the baseline: additional modality (clinical notes) and a more expressive structured model (XGBoost vs. logistic regression). Any performance gain over the baseline could be attributed entirely to XGBoost outperforming logistic regression on structured features, without any contribution from clinical notes or ensemble combination. A proper baseline battery would include: (1) XGBoost alone on structured features, (2) logistic regression on notes alone, and (3) XGBoost alone to isolate the ensemble contribution.

The TF-IDF vectorization of clinical notes with 500 features captures bag-of-words statistics but discards clinical note structure, negation, temporal context within notes, and semantic relationships. For readmission prediction, negated findings and temporal markers within notes carry distinct predictive signals that TF-IDF cannot represent. The choice of 500 TF-IDF features is not ablated against alternatives.
all_issues_raised: Fixed equal-weight averaging of XGBoost and logistic regression outputs is not principled — it assumes equal predictive scale and complementarity that is not validated, and may underperform a properly weighted or stacked ensemble.

The baseline (logistic regression on structured data only) does not isolate the ensemble contribution from the XGBoost architectural advantage; a standalone XGBoost baseline is required to attribute performance gains to ensemble combination versus model selection.

TF-IDF with 500 features discards negation, temporal context, and semantic structure in clinical notes; the notes modality is likely underutilized, and no ablation tests whether richer representations improve performance.

round_transcripts.critic:
The ensemble combines XGBoost on structured features with logistic regression on TF-IDF clinical note features, fused via a fixed equal-weight average. Equal-weight averaging is not a principled ensemble strategy — it assumes the two component models have identical predictive scale and error distributions on the target variable, which is empirically unlikely when one model uses dense structured features and the other uses high-dimensional sparse text features. Without learned ensemble weights or stacking, the ensemble cannot adapt to cases where one modality is more informative, and the fixed combination may underperform a properly weighted or stacked ensemble while also underperforming the stronger individual component.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on structured data only, which is not a comparable baseline to the full ensemble. The ensemble has two informational advantages over the baseline: additional modality (clinical notes) and a more expressive structured model (XGBoost vs. logistic regression). Any performance gain over the baseline could be attributed entirely to XGBoost outperforming logistic regression on structured features, without any contribution from clinical notes or ensemble combination. A proper baseline battery would include: (1) XGBoost alone on structured features, (2) logistic regression on notes alone, and (3) XGBoost alone to isolate the ensemble contribution.

The TF-IDF vectorization of clinical notes with 500 features captures bag-of-words statistics but discards clinical note structure, negation, temporal context within notes, and semantic relationships. For readmission prediction, negated findings and temporal markers within notes carry distinct predictive signals that TF-IDF cannot represent. The choice of 500 TF-IDF features is not ablated against alternatives.
round_transcripts.defender:
Equal-weight averaging is a well-established ensemble baseline precisely because it avoids the overfitting risk of learned ensemble weights on a validation set — with 10,000 validation records and a 15-20% positive rate, estimating a stacking weight introduces variance that can exceed its bias-reduction benefit. The equal-weight combination is a principled choice for an initial ensemble evaluation. If the two component models have similar AUROC (which would be expected given that structured features dominate readmission prediction), equal weights are close to optimal.

On the baseline comparison, the hypothesis explicitly tests whether combining historical treatment data with clinical notes outperforms historical data alone. The baseline represents the current clinical practice standard — logistic regression on structured EHR data — and the ensemble represents the proposed improvement. Decomposing the sources of improvement (XGBoost vs. LR on structured, notes vs. no notes) is a valid secondary analysis but does not invalidate the primary comparison that addresses the stated hypothesis.

TF-IDF is a practical choice for a first evaluation of clinical note utility. It is interpretable, computationally efficient, and has demonstrated utility in clinical NLP tasks. The 500-feature limit is a regularization decision. If TF-IDF notes contribute to ensemble performance above the structured-only baseline, that is evidence that clinical notes add value; a richer representation would only strengthen this evidence.

round_transcripts.critic: The defense does not address the baseline gap: whether clinical notes add value cannot be determined without a baseline that includes XGBoost on structured features alone. If XGBoost alone matches or exceeds the ensemble, the clinical notes contribute nothing and the hypothesis is not supported regardless of ensemble performance relative to logistic regression.
round_transcripts.defender: The hypothesis frames clinical notes as adding value over historical treatment data alone — if XGBoost on structured data matches the ensemble, that would falsify the hypothesis, but the experiment is designed to detect the positive case. The baseline adequately tests the hypothesis as stated; additional ablations are extensions, not requirements.

all_issues_adjudicated: The baseline (logistic regression on structured data only) does not isolate the ensemble contribution from the XGBoost architectural advantage; a standalone XGBoost baseline is required to attribute performance gains to ensemble combination versus model selection.
